lesson.menu_desc: Read and discuss contract clauses: shall for obligation, must for requirement, may for permission -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Contracts and Agreements -- Legal English
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Claudia Curimbaba | Aula 16 -- Contracts and Agreements

listenings.text: Good morning. Before you sign anything, let me walk you through the key clauses of this agreement. First, the obligations. Each party shall deliver its part of the project on time, and the supplier must meet the quality standards in the annex. These are firm duties; they are not optional. Second, the permissions. Either party may request a review meeting once a quarter, and the buyer may inspect the goods before payment. Third, the protections. The supplier shall indemnify the buyer against any third-party claim, and the warranty must cover defects for twelve months. Finally, the exit. Either party may terminate the contract with sixty days written notice. If one side commits a serious breach, the other may terminate immediately. Remember: this contract is binding once both parties sign it. Please read every provision carefully before we proceed.

Before the call with our US partner, I want to be clear about the language of the contract. The strong words carry the real weight. When the text says each party shall deliver, that is a firm obligation, a duty we cannot avoid. When it says the supplier must meet the quality standards, that is a hard requirement. And when it says either party may request a meeting, that is only a permission, something we are allowed to do, not forced to do. I also need to watch the protection clauses. We shall indemnify them only for our own faults, not for theirs, and the warranty must be limited to twelve months. If they want to amend any provision, we will negotiate it before signing. Once we both sign, the agreement is binding, so every word matters.

extra_audio.text: First, you read the whole agreement once, from start to finish, to understand the full picture. Then, you find the obligation clauses, the duties marked with shall and must. After that, you check the permission clauses, the rights marked with may. Next, you study the protection clauses, the warranty and the indemnity. Finally, you confirm how each party may terminate the contract, and only then you sign.
